# Blog topic queue

One topic per line. Every morning the draft workflow takes the first unchecked
topic, writes a post in Ali's voice, and opens a PR. Checked topics have been
used. Add new ideas anywhere in the list; blank lines and comments are ignored.

- [ ] The hidden cost of "we'll fix it after launch"
- [ ] Why the best requirement documents are written after talking to the people who do the work
- [ ] What restaurants taught me about real-time systems
- [ ] AI will not replace your team, but it will change what "junior" means
- [ ] The art of the honest status update
- [ ] Why every migration plan needs a rollback story
- [ ] Training people on software: why patience beats documentation
- [ ] The difference between a demo and a product
- [ ] Why I never trust a demo that goes perfectly
- [ ] The most expensive phrase in software: "while we're at it"
- [ ] What a busy Friday service teaches you about load testing
- [ ] The quiet power of a well-written handover document
- [ ] Vendor lock-in is a relationship, not a trap
- [ ] Why the second week of a rollout matters more than the first
- [ ] The meeting that should have been a screenshot
- [ ] Legacy systems are institutional memory with bad documentation
- [ ] The junior engineer's superpower: asking the obvious question
- [ ] Why "the system is slow" is never the whole ticket
- [ ] Estimates are promises about uncertainty, not deadlines
- [ ] On-call is a design review you cannot ignore
- [ ] Why cadence beats heroics in software delivery
- [ ] What changes when your client can text you directly
- [ ] Reading a room is a technical skill
- [ ] Why we write the rollback plan before the feature
- [ ] The hidden curriculum of enterprise IT: who to ask, not what to ask
- [ ] Small businesses deserve enterprise-grade thinking at small-business prices
- [ ] What my sons taught me about receiving code review
- [ ] The art of saying no to a feature request without losing the client
